summary: This transcript captures a system design interview for LinkedIn's job search feature. The candidate, Shiali, begins by introducing herself and her experience. The interviewer then tasks her with designing LinkedIn, which she quickly scopes down to the job seeker's perspective, focusing on finding jobs and incorporating AI for recommendations. Shiali outlines functional and non-functional requirements, including low latency and eventual consistency, and scales the system for daily active users and job postings. She then proposes an API design and a high-level architecture involving an API Gateway, Search Service, Job Manager, User Profile Database, Jobs Data Store (NoSQL), and an Elastic Search for active jobs. The discussion delves into ranking with a Personalization Service backed by GenAI/LLMs, latency optimization with caching and fault tolerance, and cache sizing. Finally, they explore simplified AI-centric architectures and an 'I'm feeling lucky' auto-apply feature.

Now, in terms of the performance and making it fault-tolerant and also highly available, the things that I would like to do is making sure, first, is that these systems have, for example, our Job Data Search, the user profile, everything has a replica so that the data which user is storing with us is not lost. So that is one thing. Also, I would like to make it available in different regions so that even if one availability zone fails, I don't want it to, I mean, just go away. So probably separating that out also helps. And also, LinkedIn is a global service, so if we have to also handle for different geographies, they will have different scales. It's better that we separate out their data, and also they may have different policies, for example, the GDPR in the EU region, they want the data to be localized versus all those things. So bringing in that compliance angle as well, I want to keep that data separately also in their in-house data stores or somewhere else, whichever is needed for that region. So that way, also the impact will not be there across geographies. So that is there for the performance. The another thing is, even in terms of the Ranking Service, let's say, in case of some issues, let's say the Personalization Service is not able to actually personalize the data. So one option is, of course, I keep on waiting on that and do nothing, right? I mean, fail the request, not able to show the relevant results, which is not, of course, a good user experience. In that case, I may want to just default back to the common, to the simpler logic which I called out earlier, for example, rank it by the latest job date, maybe. So very simpler logics. So I also want to put in these levers into my system, like what if this fails, I can do this kind of a system. So yeah, I think these.

training_pairs.question: Yeah, so, can you, I'm just trying to make sure I clearly understand the difference between the Jobs Data Store and the Job Data. Can you maybe just re-clarify that for me?
training_pairs.answer: So, okay, so this is more like all the job-related information which is being added as soon as the job is created. So this will have all the details: when it got created, what is the job status currently, is it active, not active, or it got closed, and something whenever any update happens, whenever any delete happens, right? Now, this Job Data Elastic Search, as soon as a job gets created and it's in the active position, it will get sent to this Elastic Search. And similarly, when the jobs are expired or get deleted, it will also get deleted from this Elastic Search. But in this, we are not storing the entire job details. Like, for example, a job will have a lot of other details as well, right? Like, for example, the things which are even not, like, for example, how to, the apply link for a job, right? Or the things which are not relevant in terms of the search, but then are the metadata related to the job. Like, for example, the apply link, or for example, how it should look like, or some image-related data, etc. So, but everything related to it is stored in this. But in this Elastic Search, we are only storing the fields which are searchable or which are like tags. I mean, one way of saying it is, can refer to as tags for that, right? So we're tagging like this job ID is in this location and is for this job role, and also for maybe for this organization. Let's assume these are the only three fields for now where the search is possibly can happen. So all that data is indexed in this Elastic Search and alongside a job ID. So whenever someone is searching for a job, so I search from it to get the faster results, and then maybe decorate with more database, so that more data from my Jobs Data Store to show it back to the user.

training_pairs.question: How would you set up an enterprise version of OpenAI to be able to access all this LinkedIn data? So let's say we're LinkedIn and we're licensing an instance of OpenAI, but we don't want to give our data to, you know, to ChatGPT, the public cloud version. We want to have this in-house. What would that look like? How would that manifest?
training_pairs.answer: So yeah, I mean, we exactly, we need to have our inbuilt modeling built for it because it will also be customized for our use case, right? I mean, we don't want it to return something which is out of the context, right? And not, I mean, maybe good for the general context, but not in the LinkedIn context. So I want everything to be in-house. So every, the information, the various information that I'm capturing in LinkedIn from user activities, everything, all of that will reside in my local in-house large language models. And it's like I'm storing all that data, training my model on it, and also taking in the feedback again and again. For example, if I'm returning some results, is the user finding it relevant or not? I may not need to ask them directly whether you're finding it relevant or not. I can also use their signals. For example, if they're clicking and going and applying for a job, probably they are finding it relevant. If they're not doing so, have not taken any actions, it's not relevant for them, and there could be further chance of improving it. So I capture all this data and put it back in my feedback loop. So it will be something that we have to develop in the context of LinkedIn, and in-house is something I'm thinking.
